title: Ervatamia baviensis
intro: Ervatamia baviensis, also known as Vietnamese Evodia, is a plant species in the family Rutaceae, commonly found in Southeast Asia. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant activities that contribute to its potential in treating various health conditions such as fever, cough, and respiratory infections. The bioactive constituents of Ervatamia baviensis include alkaloids, such as evodiamine and rutaecarpine, which have been shown to possess pharmacological activities. In traditional medicine, Ervatamia baviensis is often used to make herbal preparations such as teas, infusions, and decoctions, which are used to treat a range of health issues, particularly respiratory and gastrointestinal problems.
taxonomy: Ervatamia baviensis is classified within the family Apocynaceae, a group of flowering plants commonly known as the dogbane or oleander family. It belongs to the order Gentianales, which includes other flowering plant families such as the gentian and logania families. Within the Apocynaceae family, Ervatamia baviensis is placed in the subfamily Apoideae. This subfamily includes many other plant genera, such as Vinca and Catharanthus. Ervatamia baviensis is a member of the Apocynaceae family, which comprises a diverse range of flowering plant species.

side_effects: Ervatamia baviensis, also known as the 'cat's claw vine', is a plant with various traditional uses in medicine. However, due to a lack of extensive research, there is limited information available on its side effects. Some studies have reported that Ervatamia baviensis may cause adverse reactions such as allergic contact dermatitis in sensitive individuals. Additionally, long-term use of the plant may lead to gastrointestinal issues, including stomach upset and diarrhea.
